market_score_rounded.description: The market score rounded to the nearest whole number, producing an integer from 1 to 10. This is the display-ready version used on the FutureProof compare screen. Null only if market_score is null (currently 0 rows).

confidence_tier.description: A three-level classification of how reliable the data is for career guidance purposes. 'high' means this is a specific, well-defined occupation with wage data available — guidance based on this row will be targeted and precise. 'medium' means this is a broad or catch-all occupation category with wage data — the data is real but represents a heterogeneous group, so guidance will be less specific. 'low' means wage data is unavailable (23 occupations) — the ERN stat cannot be computed, limiting the usefulness for salary-related guidance. Every row receives a tier.
confidence_tier.source_column: Derived: from wage_available, broad_occupation_flag, and catchall_flag (see confidence tier derivation rules)

data_completeness.description: A simple measure of how much core data is available for this occupation, expressed as a proportion from 0.0 to 1.0. It counts how many of the four core fields (median_annual_wage, employment_current, employment_change_pct, openings_annual_avg) have non-null values, then divides by 4. For example, a value of 0.75 means 3 of the 4 core fields have data (typically missing only wage data). In the current dataset, values are either 0.75 (23 occupations missing wage data) or 1.0 (809 occupations with all core data present). Every row has a value.
data_completeness.source_column: Derived: count of non-null core fields / 4

backs_stats.description: A documentation field listing which FutureProof pentagon stats this occupation data product feeds. The value is always 'ERN,GRW' for every row in this table — this data product contributes to the Earnings stat (via median_annual_wage and wage percentiles) and the Growth stat (via grw_score). The remaining pentagon stats (RES, HMN) come from the O*NET Gold product.

backs_bosses.description: A documentation field listing which FutureProof boss fights this occupation data product feeds. The value is always 'Market,Ceiling' for every row in this table — this data product contributes to the Market boss fight (via market_score and growth_category) and the Ceiling boss fight (via median_annual_wage and wage_percentile_education_tier). The remaining boss fights (AI, Burnout) come from the O*NET Gold product.

source_load_date.description: The date on which the source BLS data was loaded into the pipeline's raw zone. This tells you when the data was fetched from BLS, not when the underlying employment projections were generated. Useful for tracking data freshness and identifying which load batch a row belongs to. Carried from Silver without modification.

promoted_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was promoted to the Gold zone consumable table. This is generated automatically at Gold promotion time and replaces Silver's ingested_at timestamp. It tells you when the Gold transformation ran, not when the source data was collected.

raw.onet_occupations.description: Master reference table of all occupations in the O*NET 30.2 database. Each row represents one occupation identified by an O*NET-SOC code, with its title and a narrative description of what people in that occupation do. This is the lookup table that all other O*NET tables join to -- every task statement, work activity rating, work context rating, and related occupation reference points back to this table via the onet_soc_code field. O*NET is the third primary data source for FutureProof, providing the task-level and work-environment data that powers AI exposure scoring (RES stat), human-skill profiling (HMN stat), burnout assessment, and career branching.
raw.onet_occupations.source: O*NET 30.2 Database — Occupation Data.txt (bulk ZIP download from onetcenter.org)

onet_soc_code.description: The O*NET-SOC code that uniquely identifies this occupation. O*NET uses an extended version of the Standard Occupational Classification system with the format XX-XXXX.XX -- the first six digits (XX-XXXX) match the BLS SOC code for the occupation, and the two-digit suffix after the period distinguishes O*NET-specific detailed occupations within a single BLS occupation. For example, 15-1252.00 is the base code for Software Developers (matching BLS 15-1252), while 29-1229.01 and 29-1229.02 split BLS's 'Physicians, All Other' into more specific roles. Of the 1,016 occupations in O*NET 30.2, 867 are base codes ending in .00 and 149 are detailed splits. This field is the grain of the table and the primary join key for all other O*NET tables.
onet_soc_code.cde_rationale: Primary identifier and grain field for the O*NET occupation master table. Join key for all 4 other O*NET raw tables and the cross-source bridge to BLS OOH via 6-digit SOC truncation. Every downstream FutureProof analysis depends on this field.
onet_soc_code.notes: 1,016 distinct codes. 867 are base BLS codes (.00), 149 are O*NET detailed splits. Format: XX-XXXX.XX. Silver zone will truncate to 6-digit XX-XXXX for BLS joining while preserving full codes for O*NET-internal joins.

title.description: The official name of the occupation as defined by O*NET. For example, 'Software Developers' or 'Registered Nurses.' Each O*NET-SOC code maps to exactly one title. This is the human-readable label that appears in FutureProof's career guidance outputs, Gold zone products, and MCP grounding documents. While onet_soc_code is the join key for computation, title is what makes occupation data meaningful to end users.
title.cde_rationale: Human-readable occupation name required for all consumer-facing outputs including career guidance UI, Gold zone products, and MCP grounding documents. A null or incorrect title renders the data product unusable.
title.notes: 1:1 mapping with onet_soc_code. All 1,016 values are non-null.

description.description: A narrative definition of what people in this occupation do, written by O*NET occupational analysts. For example, the description for Software Developers explains the types of software they create and the activities they perform. Descriptions range from 25 to 811 characters. This text feeds Gemma career descriptions as supplementary context but is not a join key or computational input -- the occupation can be identified and analyzed using title and task data alone.
description.notes: Length range 25-811 characters. All 1,016 values are non-null. Provides supplementary context for career guidance but is not analytically critical.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was loaded into the pipeline by the O*NET ingestor. This is pipeline metadata, not a property of the source data. All rows in a single batch share the same ingested_at value.

source_url.description: The URL from which the O*NET ZIP archive was downloaded. This is pipeline metadata that records provenance -- which specific download produced this row. All rows in a batch share the same value.

source_method.description: The method used to acquire the data. For O*NET tables, this is always 'bulk_zip_download', meaning the data was obtained by downloading a ZIP archive from the O*NET Resource Center and extracting the relevant text files.

load_date.description: The calendar date when this batch of data was loaded into the pipeline. This is pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring -- the DQ rules check that load_date is within 30 days of the current date to ensure the data is not stale.

raw.onet_task_statements.description: Task-level work descriptions for each O*NET occupation. Each row represents one specific task that workers in a particular occupation perform, such as 'Analyze user needs and software requirements to determine feasibility of design.' This table is the primary input for FutureProof's AI automation exposure scoring (RES stat) and the AI boss fight -- each task is evaluated for whether AI could perform it, and the results are aggregated to produce an occupation-level AI exposure profile. The table contains 18,796 task statements across 923 occupations, with an average of about 20 tasks per occupation (ranging from 5 to 30+).
raw.onet_task_statements.source: O*NET 30.2 Database — Task Statements.txt (bulk ZIP download from onetcenter.org)

onet_soc_code.description: The O*NET-SOC code identifying which occupation this task belongs to. This is a foreign key to raw.onet_occupations and part of the table's grain. Every task must be associated with an occupation so that AI exposure scoring can be attributed to the correct career. Format: XX-XXXX.XX.
onet_soc_code.cde_rationale: Grain field and foreign key to raw.onet_occupations. Required to link task data to occupations for AI exposure scoring (RES stat) and AI boss fight. Also enables cross-source integration via SOC truncation to BLS OOH.
onet_soc_code.notes: 923 distinct SOC codes. All verified to exist in raw.onet_occupations (0 orphans).

task_id.description: A numeric identifier that uniquely identifies this specific task across the entire O*NET database. Unlike onet_soc_code, which can repeat across rows (one occupation has many tasks), task_id is globally unique -- no two tasks share the same ID, even across different occupations. This is the atomic unit for AI automation exposure scoring: each task_id represents one discrete work task that will be evaluated for 'can AI do this?' The ID is stable across O*NET releases, allowing tracking of individual tasks over time.
task_id.cde_rationale: Part of the table grain (onet_soc_code x task_id) and the atomic unit for AI automation exposure scoring. Each task_id is independently evaluated for AI susceptibility. Globally unique across all 18,796 tasks.
task_id.notes: 18,796 distinct values (100% globally unique). Integer type in source.

task.description: The plain-English description of a specific work task performed in this occupation. For example, a Software Developer task might be 'Analyze user needs and software requirements to determine feasibility of design within time and cost constraints.' This text is the core analytical content of the table -- it is the input to AI automation susceptibility scoring, where each task description is evaluated (potentially via LLM semantic matching) to determine how automatable it is. Task descriptions range from 13 to 332 characters.
task.cde_rationale: The actual task description text is the input to AI automation susceptibility scoring. Directly powers the RES stat and AI boss fight. Without task text, the occupation's AI exposure profile cannot be computed.
task.notes: Length range: 13-332 characters, mean 98. All 18,796 values non-null and non-empty.

task_type.description: A classification of the task as 'Core' (the main tasks that define the occupation, 72.6% of rows), 'Supplemental' (additional tasks that some workers perform, 22.9%), or 'n/a' (analyst-derived tasks where the classification does not apply, 4.5%). The 'n/a' values correlate exactly with domain_source='Analyst'. Both Core and Supplemental tasks are used in AI exposure scoring -- this field is useful for filtering or weighting but is not itself a primary analytical input.
task_type.notes: Valid values: Core (72.6%), Supplemental (22.9%), n/a (4.5%). The 'n/a' values correlate 1:1 with domain_source='Analyst'.

incumbents_responding.description: The number of workers (survey respondents) who confirmed that this task is part of their job. This provides a confidence indicator for the task data -- tasks with more respondents are more reliably established as part of the occupation. Values range from 18 to 238 when present, but 6.3% of rows are null because analyst-derived and analyst-transition tasks have no survey respondents. This field is useful for data quality assessment but is not a direct input to FutureProof's AI exposure scoring.
incumbents_responding.notes: Range: 18-238 when populated. Null for 6.3% of rows (Analyst and Analyst-Transition domain sources).

date.description: The date when this task data was collected from survey respondents or analysts, stored in MM/YYYY format. This indicates how recently the occupation's task list was reviewed. Data collection dates range from December 2004 to December 2025, reflecting O*NET's rolling update cycle where different occupations are surveyed at different times.
date.notes: Format: MM/YYYY. Range: 12/2004 to 12/2025. Reflects O*NET's rolling update cycle.

domain_source.description: Who provided this task data: 'Incumbent' (70.2%) means working professionals described their own tasks via survey; 'Occupational Expert' (23.5%) means O*NET analysts defined the task; 'Analyst' (4.5%) means an O*NET analyst created the task without survey data; 'Analyst-Transition' (1.8%) means an analyst created tasks for a transitioning occupation. The domain source affects data quality characteristics (e.g., analyst tasks have no survey sample size) but is not itself an analytical input for FutureProof.
domain_source.notes: Values: Incumbent (70.2%), Occupational Expert (23.5%), Analyst (4.5%), Analyst-Transition (1.8%). Affects data quality characteristics.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was loaded into the pipeline by the O*NET ingestor. Pipeline metadata -- all rows in a batch share the same value.

source_url.description: The URL from which the O*NET ZIP archive was downloaded. Pipeline metadata recording provenance.

source_method.description: The method used to acquire the data. Always 'bulk_zip_download' for O*NET tables.

load_date.description: The calendar date when this batch of data was loaded into the pipeline. Pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring.

raw.onet_work_activities.description: Quantitative ratings of 41 generalized work activities for each O*NET occupation, measuring both how important each activity is (Importance scale, 1-5) and at what level it is performed (Level scale, 0-7). For example, 'Getting Information' might have an importance of 4.2 and a level of 5.1 for Software Developers. These ratings are the primary input for FutureProof's HMN (Human Edge) stat, which profiles how much human-skill intensity an occupation requires across dimensions like critical thinking, interpersonal interaction, and physical activity. The table contains 73,308 rows across 894 occupations.
raw.onet_work_activities.source: O*NET 30.2 Database — Work Activities.txt (bulk ZIP download from onetcenter.org)

onet_soc_code.description: The O*NET-SOC code identifying which occupation this work activity rating belongs to. This is a foreign key to raw.onet_occupations and part of the table's grain. Each occupation has ratings for all 41 generalized work activities on both the Importance and Level scales, producing about 82 rows per occupation. Format: XX-XXXX.XX.
onet_soc_code.cde_rationale: Grain field and foreign key to raw.onet_occupations. Required to link work activity ratings to occupations for the HMN (Human Edge) stat calculation. Also enables cross-source integration via SOC truncation to BLS OOH.
onet_soc_code.notes: 894 distinct SOC codes. All verified to exist in raw.onet_occupations (0 orphans). 122 fewer occupations than the master table (occupations not yet surveyed for work activities).

element_id.description: The O*NET Content Model element identifier that specifies which of the 41 generalized work activities is being rated. Element IDs follow a hierarchical numbering scheme (for example, '4.A.1.a.1' for 'Getting Information' or '4.A.4.a.4' for 'Establishing and Maintaining Interpersonal Relationships'). This is part of the table's grain -- combined with onet_soc_code and scale_id, it uniquely identifies each rating. The 41 activity elements represent the complete taxonomy of generalized human work activities defined by O*NET.
element_id.cde_rationale: Part of the table grain and the structural key that maps to specific human-skill dimensions in the HMN stat. Without correct element_ids, activity ratings cannot be mapped to the right skill dimensions. The 41 fixed elements are the backbone of the activity rating system.
element_id.notes: 41 distinct element IDs. Hierarchical format (e.g., '4.A.1.a.1'). Fixed taxonomy -- same 41 elements for every occupation.

element_name.description: The human-readable name of the work activity being rated, such as 'Getting Information', 'Making Decisions and Solving Problems', or 'Performing General Physical Activities.' Each element_id maps to exactly one element_name. This is a display label for reporting and exploration -- element_id is the computational key used in joins and scoring.

scale_id.description: Identifies which measurement scale was used for this rating: 'IM' (Importance) measures how important the activity is to the occupation on a 1-5 scale, while 'LV' (Level) measures the complexity or sophistication at which the activity is performed on a 0-7 scale. Each occupation-activity combination has two rows -- one IM and one LV -- which is why the table is perfectly balanced at 50% IM / 50% LV. Part of the table's grain.
scale_id.notes: Exactly 2 values: IM (50%) and LV (50%). Determines valid range of data_value.

data_value.description: The actual numeric rating for this work activity. On the Importance scale (IM), values range from 1.00 to 4.99 where higher means more important to the occupation. On the Level scale (LV), values range from 0.00 to 6.81 where higher means the activity is performed at greater complexity. These are continuous double-precision values, not rounded integers -- the decimal precision matters because they are aggregated across all 41 activities to produce the occupation's human-skill profile for the HMN stat. A value of 0.00 on the LV scale indicates the activity is not relevant to the occupation.
data_value.cde_rationale: The core measurement in this table. Quantitative input to the HMN (Human Edge) stat and AI boss fight. Ratings are aggregated across 41 activities per occupation to produce human-skill profiles. Precision matters -- values are continuous doubles.
data_value.notes: IM scale: 1.00-4.99. LV scale: 0.00-6.81. Full double precision preserved.

n.description: The number of survey respondents whose ratings were averaged to produce the data_value for this activity. Provides a confidence indicator -- ratings based on more respondents are more reliable. Range is 9-99 when populated, but 1.8% of rows are null because Analyst-Transition sources have no survey respondents.
n.notes: Range: 9-99 when populated. Null for 1.8% of rows (Analyst-Transition sources).

standard_error.description: The standard error of the mean rating estimate, indicating the statistical precision of the data_value. Smaller values mean more precise estimates. Null for 25.3% of rows where no sampling distribution applies (Occupational Expert and Analyst-Transition sources). This is statistical metadata, not an analytical input for FutureProof.
standard_error.notes: Null for 25.3% of rows (Occupational Expert and Analyst-Transition sources).

lower_ci_bound.description: The lower bound of the confidence interval for the rating estimate. Together with upper_ci_bound, this indicates the range within which the true rating likely falls. Null for 25.3% of rows (same pattern as standard_error). Statistical metadata, not used in FutureProof's scoring.

upper_ci_bound.description: The upper bound of the confidence interval for the rating estimate. Together with lower_ci_bound, this indicates the range within which the true rating likely falls. Null for 25.3% of rows (same pattern as standard_error). Statistical metadata, not used in FutureProof's scoring.

recommend_suppress.description: An O*NET data quality flag indicating whether the rating should be treated with caution. 'N' (74.9%) means the data is reliable; 'n/a' (23.6%) means the flag does not apply for this source type (expert or analyst data); 'Y' (1.5%) means O*NET recommends suppressing the value due to small sample size or other quality concerns. The Silver zone should filter or flag rows where this is 'Y'. This is a data quality indicator, not an analytical value.
recommend_suppress.notes: Values: N (74.9%), n/a (23.6%), Y (1.5%). Silver zone should filter rows where Y.

not_relevant.description: A relevance flag that only applies to Level (LV) scale rows. 'N' (48.5%) means the activity is relevant to the occupation at the measured level; 'Y' (1.5%) means the activity is not relevant to this occupation (data_value near zero, 0.00-1.79); 'n/a' (50%) covers all Importance (IM) rows where this flag does not apply. The Silver zone should interpret LV rows with not_relevant='Y' as indicating the occupation does not meaningfully involve this activity.
not_relevant.notes: Values: n/a (50% -- all IM rows), N (48.5% -- LV relevant), Y (1.5% -- LV not relevant, data_value near zero).

date.description: The date when this work activity rating was collected from survey respondents or analysts, stored in MM/YYYY format. Data collection dates range from December 2005 to August 2025, reflecting O*NET's rolling update cycle.

domain_source.description: Who provided this rating: 'Incumbent' (74.7%) means working professionals rated the activity via survey; 'Occupational Expert' (23.5%) means O*NET analysts provided the rating; 'Analyst-Transition' (1.8%) means an analyst rated a transitioning occupation. Affects data quality characteristics (sample size, statistical measures) but is not an analytical input for FutureProof.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was loaded into the pipeline by the O*NET ingestor. Pipeline metadata.

source_url.description: The URL from which the O*NET ZIP archive was downloaded. Pipeline metadata recording provenance.

source_method.description: The method used to acquire the data. Always 'bulk_zip_download' for O*NET tables.

load_date.description: The calendar date when this batch of data was loaded into the pipeline. Pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring.

raw.onet_work_context.description: Quantitative ratings of 57 work context dimensions for each O*NET occupation, describing the physical, social, and structural environment in which work is performed. This includes dimensions like time pressure, hours worked per week, physical proximity to others, exposure to hazards, and work schedule regularity. This table is the primary input for FutureProof's Burnout boss fight, which assesses how stressful or demanding a work environment is. The table is more complex than Work Activities because it uses four different scale types: CX (5-point estimates), CT (3-point estimates), CXP (5-category percentages), and CTP (3-category percentages). The percentage scales produce multiple rows per element (one per response category), resulting in 297,676 total rows -- significantly more than the spec estimate of ~49,000.
raw.onet_work_context.source: O*NET 30.2 Database — Work Context.txt (bulk ZIP download from onetcenter.org)

onet_soc_code.description: The O*NET-SOC code identifying which occupation this work context rating belongs to. This is a foreign key to raw.onet_occupations and part of the table's grain. Each occupation has ratings across all 57 work context dimensions, with multiple rows per dimension for percentage-based scales. Format: XX-XXXX.XX.
onet_soc_code.cde_rationale: Grain field and foreign key to raw.onet_occupations. Required to link work context data to occupations for the Burnout boss fight calculation. Also enables cross-source integration via SOC truncation to BLS OOH.
onet_soc_code.notes: 894 distinct SOC codes. All verified to exist in raw.onet_occupations (0 orphans). Same 122-occupation gap as Work Activities.

element_id.description: The O*NET Content Model element identifier specifying which of the 57 work context dimensions is being rated. Element IDs follow a hierarchical numbering scheme (for example, '4.C.1.a.2.a' for 'Time Pressure' or '4.C.3.d.4' for 'Duration of Typical Work Week'). Key burnout-relevant elements include Time Pressure, Duration of Typical Work Week, Work Schedules, and Physical Proximity. The 57 elements define the complete work environment taxonomy used by O*NET.
element_id.cde_rationale: Part of the table grain and the structural key that identifies which of the 57 work context dimensions is being measured. Key burnout-relevant elements (Time Pressure, Work Schedules, etc.) are selected by element_id for the Burnout boss fight.
element_id.notes: 57 distinct element IDs. Hierarchical format (e.g., '4.C.1.a.2.a'). Fixed taxonomy.

element_name.description: The human-readable name of the work context dimension being rated, such as 'Time Pressure', 'Physical Proximity', or 'Duration of Typical Work Week.' Each element_id maps to exactly one element_name. This is a display label -- element_id is the computational key.

scale_id.description: Identifies which measurement scale was used for this rating. Work Context uses four scales: CX (context 5-point estimate, 16.5% of rows) provides a single rating on a 1-5 scale; CT (context 3-point estimate, 0.6%) provides a single rating on a 1-3 scale; CXP (context 5-category percentage, 81.1%) gives the percentage of respondents choosing each of 5 response categories; CTP (context 3-category percentage, 1.8%) gives percentages for 3 categories. The CX/CT point estimates are the most directly useful for burnout scoring; CXP/CTP provide richer distributional detail.
scale_id.notes: 4 values: CXP (81.1%), CX (16.5%), CTP (1.8%), CT (0.6%). Determines valid range and interpretation of data_value.

data_value.description: The actual numeric rating for this work context dimension. The interpretation depends on the scale_id: on CX (1.00-5.00), it is a point estimate of the context dimension's intensity (e.g., time pressure level); on CT (1.00-3.00), it is a point estimate on a 3-point scale; on CXP/CTP (0.00-100.00), it is the percentage of respondents who chose a particular response category. The CX point estimates are the critical analytical values for FutureProof's burnout scoring -- for example, a high Time Pressure CX value indicates a high-stress occupation.
data_value.cde_rationale: The core measurement in this table. CX point estimates directly quantify work environment dimensions used for the Burnout boss fight. CXP/CTP percentages provide distributional detail. Precision matters for scoring.

n.description: The number of survey respondents whose ratings were averaged to produce the data_value. Range is 12-99 when populated. Null for only 0.3% of rows (Analyst-Transition sources). A higher sample size generally indicates a more reliable estimate.
n.notes: Range: 12-99 when populated. Null for 0.3% of rows (Analyst-Transition sources).

standard_error.description: The standard error of the mean rating estimate. Null for 24.2% of rows (Occupational Expert and Analyst-Transition sources). CXP percentage values can have high standard errors (up to 34.01) because percentage distributions are inherently more variable. Statistical metadata, not an analytical input.
standard_error.notes: Null for 24.2%. CXP percentages can have high SE (max 34.01).

lower_ci_bound.description: The lower bound of the confidence interval for the rating estimate. Null pattern matches standard_error. Statistical metadata.

upper_ci_bound.description: The upper bound of the confidence interval for the rating estimate. Null pattern matches standard_error. Statistical metadata.

recommend_suppress.description: An O*NET data quality flag indicating whether the rating should be treated with caution. Values are 'N' (73-76%, reliable), 'n/a' (23-24%, not applicable for expert/analyst sources), and 'Y' (up to 5%, O*NET recommends suppression). The suppression rate varies by scale type -- CXP/CTP percentage scales have higher suppression rates than CX/CT point-estimate scales. Total 'Y' rate across all rows is 2.5%. The Silver zone should filter or flag these rows.
recommend_suppress.notes: Total Y rate: 2.5%. Higher on CXP/CTP scales than CX/CT.

not_relevant.description: Always 'n/a' in Work Context (100% of 297,676 rows). Unlike Work Activities, Work Context never uses this relevance flag -- all 57 context dimensions are considered relevant to every occupation (though their intensity varies). Preserved in the schema for structural consistency across O*NET rating tables.
not_relevant.notes: Always 'n/a' (100%). Preserved for schema consistency, not analytically meaningful.

date.description: The date when this work context rating was collected, stored in MM/YYYY format. Reflects O*NET's rolling update cycle.

domain_source.description: Who provided this rating: Incumbent, Occupational Expert, or Analyst-Transition. Same meaning as in the Work Activities table -- affects data quality characteristics but is not an analytical input.

category.description: The response category number for CXP and CTP percentage rows. For CXP scales, values are 1-5 (representing the 5 response options for that context dimension); for CTP scales, values are 1-3. This field is null for CX and CT point-estimate rows (17.1% of the table), because point estimates do not have response categories. Part of the table grain -- combined with onet_soc_code, element_id, and scale_id, it uniquely identifies each row. This is a structural field that tells you which category a percentage value belongs to, not an independently meaningful analytical value.
category.notes: CXP: 1-5, CTP: 1-3. Null for CX/CT rows (17.1%). Part of grain. Source 'n/a' values coerced to NULL by ingestor.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was loaded into the pipeline by the O*NET ingestor. Pipeline metadata.

source_url.description: The URL from which the O*NET ZIP archive was downloaded. Pipeline metadata recording provenance.

source_method.description: The method used to acquire the data. Always 'bulk_zip_download' for O*NET tables.

load_date.description: The calendar date when this batch of data was loaded into the pipeline. Pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring.

raw.onet_related_occupations.description: A matrix showing which occupations are related to each other, based on O*NET's analysis of occupation similarity. For each of 923 source occupations, this table lists 20 related occupations: 10 primary relationships (the most closely related careers, split into 'Primary-Short' for ranks 1-5 and 'Primary-Long' for ranks 6-10) and 10 supplemental relationships (additional related careers, ranks 11-20). This table directly powers FutureProof's Stage 3 career branching feature, which answers the question 'where can someone in this career go next?' by recommending related occupations. The table has exactly 20 rows per source occupation (18,460 total), perfectly uniform with no exceptions.
raw.onet_related_occupations.source: O*NET 30.2 Database — Related Occupations.txt (bulk ZIP download from onetcenter.org)

onet_soc_code.description: The O*NET-SOC code of the source occupation -- the career 'from' which related occupations are being listed. This is a foreign key to raw.onet_occupations and part of the table's grain. In FutureProof's Stage 3 career branching, this is the user's current or target career, and its related_onet_soc_code entries are the 'where can I go next?' recommendations.
onet_soc_code.cde_rationale: Grain field (source occupation) and foreign key to raw.onet_occupations. The 'from' side of career pathway relationships. Required for Stage 3 career branching.
onet_soc_code.notes: 923 distinct source occupations. All verified to exist in raw.onet_occupations. Exactly 20 rows per source occupation.

related_onet_soc_code.description: The O*NET-SOC code of the related occupation -- the career 'to' which the source occupation is connected. This is the occupation being recommended as a related career path in FutureProof's Stage 3 branching. All 920 distinct related SOC codes have been verified to exist in raw.onet_occupations, ensuring that every career recommendation points to a valid, documented occupation.
related_onet_soc_code.cde_rationale: Grain field (target occupation) and the 'to' side of career pathway relationships. Each related_onet_soc_code is a potential 'next career' suggestion. Without correct target SOC codes, career recommendations point to nonexistent occupations.
related_onet_soc_code.notes: 920 distinct target occupations. All verified to exist in raw.onet_occupations.

related_index.description: The rank position of this related occupation, from 1 (most closely related) to 20 (least closely related of the listed occupations). The ranking is divided into three tiers: Primary-Short (1-5) are the most similar occupations, Primary-Long (6-10) are moderately similar, and Supplemental (11-20) are additional related occupations. The distribution is perfectly uniform -- exactly 923 rows at every index value from 1 to 20.
related_index.notes: Range: 1-20. Perfectly uniform distribution: 923 rows per index value. Primary-Short=1-5, Primary-Long=6-10, Supplemental=11-20.

is_primary.description: A flag that distinguishes primary related occupations (true, index 1-10) from supplemental ones (false, index 11-20). In FutureProof's Stage 3 career branching, primary relationships are shown as top career pathway recommendations -- these are the careers most similar to the user's current or target occupation. Supplemental relationships are used for broader career exploration or as fallback suggestions when primary options are not suitable. Derived by the ingestor from the source's Relatedness Tier column.
is_primary.cde_rationale: Key filter for Stage 3 career branching. Determines which career suggestions are highlighted as top recommendations (primary) versus used for broader exploration (supplemental). The primary/supplemental distinction directly affects the user-facing product.
is_primary.source_column: Derived from Relatedness Tier (Primary-Short/Primary-Long = true, Supplemental = false)
is_primary.notes: True for index 1-10 (50%), false for index 11-20 (50%). Derived from Relatedness Tier in source.

relatedness_tier.description: The O*NET-defined tier of the relationship, providing finer granularity than the is_primary flag. 'Primary-Short' (index 1-5, 25%) means the most closely related occupations; 'Primary-Long' (index 6-10, 25%) means moderately related primary occupations; 'Supplemental' (index 11-20, 50%) means additional related occupations. This field adds nuance beyond is_primary (which combines Primary-Short and Primary-Long into a single 'true' category), but is_primary is the field used for core career branching logic.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was loaded into the pipeline by the O*NET ingestor. Pipeline metadata.

source_url.description: The URL from which the O*NET ZIP archive was downloaded. Pipeline metadata recording provenance.

source_method.description: The method used to acquire the data. Always 'bulk_zip_download' for O*NET tables.

load_date.description: The calendar date when this batch of data was loaded into the pipeline. Pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring.

base.onet_occupations.description: Master O*NET occupation reference table at BLS SOC granularity (XX-XXXX). Each row represents one distinct occupation, aggregated from one or more O*NET detailed codes (XX-XXXX.XX). This table serves as the central dimension for the O*NET Silver tables — Activity Profiles, Context Profiles, and Career Transitions all reference it via bls_soc_code. Excludes 69 structurally empty BLS SOCs (occupations with zero data across all O*NET child tables at BLS level). The bls_soc_code is the primary join key connecting O*NET occupation data to BLS OOH employment projections and, via the CIP-SOC crosswalk, to College Scorecard academic programs.

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the BLS SOC code. This ID is stable — running the pipeline again on the same data produces the same ID for the same row. Format: 'on-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters. Useful for tracking specific occupation records across pipeline runs.

bls_soc_code.description: The 6-digit BLS Standard Occupational Classification code that uniquely identifies this occupation, in XX-XXXX format (for example, 15-1252 for Software Developers). This is the natural key of the table and the primary join key for connecting O*NET occupation data to BLS OOH employment projections and wages, and to College Scorecard programs via the CIP-SOC crosswalk. Derived by truncating the more granular O*NET-SOC code (XX-XXXX.XX) to the BLS level. 798 unique values.
bls_soc_code.cde_rationale: Natural key and primary cross-source join key. Every downstream Gold zone join (HMN stat, Burnout boss fight, career branching) keys on bls_soc_code. Also the crosswalk anchor linking O*NET to BLS OOH and College Scorecard. Incorrect or missing values would sever the occupation-to-program linkage core to FutureProof.
bls_soc_code.source_column: Derived from raw.onet_occupations.onetsoc_code (truncated to first 7 characters)
bls_soc_code.notes: Natural key. UNIQUE constraint. 798 distinct values. Format validated via regex ^\d{2}-\d{4}$.

primary_title.description: The official name of the occupation as defined by O*NET. For occupations that map directly to one O*NET code (the .00 base code), this is that code's title. For the 76 multi-detail BLS SOCs where multiple O*NET codes share the same BLS prefix, this is the title of the .00 base code (or the first detailed code alphabetically if no .00 exists). For example, 'Software Developers' or 'Registered Nurses'. This is a display label — the bls_soc_code field is the authoritative identifier.

description.description: A short paragraph describing what people in this occupation do on the job. Sourced from O*NET's standardized federal occupation descriptions. For multi-detail BLS SOCs, this is the description from the .00 base code.

onet_detail_codes.description: A JSON array listing all the more-granular O*NET-SOC codes (in XX-XXXX.XX format) that map to this BLS SOC code. For most occupations, this is a single code like ["15-1252.00"]. For the 76 multi-detail BLS SOCs, this lists 2 or more codes (for example, ["29-1229.01","29-1229.02","29-1229.03"]). This field preserves traceability back to the original O*NET detail level and is useful for understanding which source records contributed to aggregated values in child tables.
onet_detail_codes.source_column: Derived from raw.onet_occupations.onetsoc_code (collected into JSON array, sorted ascending)

onet_detail_count.description: The number of O*NET detailed codes that map to this BLS SOC code. A value of 1 means a simple 1:1 mapping. Values of 2 or more mean multiple O*NET specializations share the same BLS occupation code, and numeric values in child tables (importance ratings, context values) are averages across those specializations. Range: 1 to 10.
onet_detail_count.source_column: Derived from count of O*NET-SOC codes per BLS SOC
onet_detail_count.notes: CHECK constraint: >= 1 AND <= 10. Max observed: 10 (for BLS SOC 15-1299).

multi_detail_flag.description: True when more than one O*NET detailed code maps to this BLS SOC code (onet_detail_count > 1). Exactly 76 BLS SOCs have this flag set to true. When true, numeric values in the Activity Profiles and Context Profiles child tables are unweighted averages across the contributing O*NET detail codes rather than direct source values.

has_work_activities.description: True if any O*NET detail code for this BLS SOC has Work Activities data in the Bronze zone. Work Activities rate how important 41 human skills are for the occupation and feed the HMN (Human Edge) stat. 774 of 798 occupations have this data.
has_work_activities.source_column: Derived from existence check against raw.onet_work_activities

has_work_context.description: True if any O*NET detail code for this BLS SOC has Work Context data in the Bronze zone. Work Context rates environmental and structural conditions (like time pressure, decision frequency) and feeds the Burnout boss fight. 774 of 798 occupations have this data.
has_work_context.source_column: Derived from existence check against raw.onet_work_context

has_tasks.description: True if any O*NET detail code for this BLS SOC has Task Statements in the Bronze zone. Task Statements are free-text descriptions of specific job duties, used by the AI narrative generator (Gemma) directly from Bronze.
has_tasks.source_column: Derived from existence check against raw.onet_task_statements

has_related.description: True if any O*NET detail code for this BLS SOC has Related Occupations data in the Bronze zone. Related Occupations indicate which careers share similar skill profiles and feed the Stage 3 career branching tree.
has_related.source_column: Derived from existence check against raw.onet_related_occupations

data_completeness_tier.description: A classification of how much O*NET data is available for this occupation. 'full' means all 4 data types are present (work activities, work context, tasks, related occupations) — 774 occupations. 'partial' means some but not all data types are present — 24 occupations (these have tasks and related occupations but no activity or context data). Occupations with zero data ('none') are excluded from Silver entirely. This tier determines whether downstream Gold zone processes can compute complete stats or must apply partial-data handling.
data_completeness_tier.cde_rationale: Determines whether downstream Gold zone processes can use this occupation for full stat generation or must apply partial-data handling. Incorrect classification would cause Gold zone to silently produce stats from incomplete data, misleading career guidance outputs.
data_completeness_tier.source_column: Derived from has_work_activities, has_work_context, has_tasks, has_related flags
data_completeness_tier.notes: CHECK constraint: IN ('full', 'partial'). Distribution: 774 full, 24 partial.

source_load_date.description: The date this batch of O*NET data was loaded into the Bronze zone. Pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring. Does not represent when the O*NET surveys were conducted.

ingested_at.description: The timestamp when this row was written to the Silver zone base table. Generated automatically at transformation time. Pipeline metadata used for auditing and data freshness tracking.

base.onet_activity_profiles.description: Work Activity importance ratings per occupation at BLS SOC granularity. Each row rates how important one of 41 Generalized Work Activities is for a given occupation, on the O*NET Importance (IM) scale of 1.0 to 5.0. This table backs the HMN (Human Edge) stat in the FutureProof game system, which measures how much a career relies on distinctly human capabilities. For the 76 multi-detail BLS SOCs, importance values are unweighted averages across the contributing O*NET detailed codes. Only the IM (Importance) scale is included — the LV (Level) scale remains in Bronze for post-hackathon use.

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the combination of BLS SOC code and element ID. This ID is stable across pipeline re-runs. Format: 'wa-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters.
record_id.source_column: Derived from bls_soc_code + element_id via compute_grain_id(prefix='wa')

bls_soc_code.description: The 6-digit BLS SOC code identifying which occupation this activity rating belongs to, in XX-XXXX format. Foreign key to base.onet_occupations.bls_soc_code. Combined with element_id, this forms the composite grain of the table. 774 distinct occupations have activity profile data.
bls_soc_code.cde_rationale: FK to base.onet_occupations and primary join key for linking activity importance scores to occupations across all downstream Gold zone processes. The HMN stat and AI boss fight both key on bls_soc_code. Incorrect values would assign activity importance to the wrong occupation, distorting HMN stats.
bls_soc_code.notes: FK to base.onet_occupations. Format validated via regex ^\d{2}-\d{4}$. 774 distinct values.

element_id.description: The O*NET Content Model element identifier for the specific Work Activity being rated. Format is a dotted-hierarchical code like '4.A.1.a.1' (for 'Getting Information'). There are exactly 41 distinct element IDs representing the 41 Generalized Work Activities in the O*NET taxonomy. These IDs are stable across O*NET releases. Gold zone HMN stat computation selects specific element_ids to compute the Human Edge score.
element_id.cde_rationale: Part of the composite grain and the identifier that uniquely distinguishes which of the 41 Work Activity dimensions this row measures. Gold zone HMN stat and AI exposure analysis filter by element_id. Incorrect values would map importance scores to wrong activity dimensions, corrupting HMN stats.
element_id.notes: Part of composite natural key. Exactly 41 distinct values.

element_name.description: The human-readable name of the Work Activity, such as 'Getting Information', 'Making Decisions and Solving Problems', or 'Communicating with Supervisors, Peers, or Subordinates'. This is a display label denormalized from Bronze for convenience — the element_id is the authoritative identifier.

importance.description: A rating of how important this Work Activity is for this occupation, on the O*NET Importance (IM) scale from 1.0 (not important) to 5.0 (extremely important). For multi-detail BLS SOCs, this is the unweighted average across all contributing O*NET detailed codes. This is the primary numeric input to the Gold zone HMN (Human Edge) stat, which determines which human skills are most critical for each occupation. Also feeds the AI boss fight by identifying high-importance activities.
importance.cde_rationale: Primary numeric input to the Gold zone HMN (Human Edge) stat. Directly backs activity-level importance that determines which skills are most critical. Also feeds AI boss fight by identifying high-importance activities that may or may not be automatable. Incorrect values would directly distort HMN scores and AI exposure assessments presented to students.
importance.source_column: AVG(raw.onet_work_activities.data_value) WHERE scale_id = 'IM', grouped by bls_soc_code and element_id
importance.notes: CHECK constraint: >= 1.0 AND <= 5.0. Observed range in current data: 1.00-4.99.

importance_rank.description: The rank of this activity within the occupation, based on importance rating. Rank 1 is the most important activity, rank 41 is the least important. Every occupation has a complete 1-through-41 ranking with no gaps. Useful for quickly identifying an occupation's top skills without comparing raw importance values.
importance_rank.source_column: Derived: RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY bls_soc_code ORDER BY importance DESC)
importance_rank.notes: CHECK constraint: >= 1 AND <= 41. Complete 1-41 sequence per occupation.

is_high_importance.description: True if the importance rating is 3.5 or higher, indicating this activity is in roughly the top 40% of the 1-5 importance scale. A convenience flag for quick filtering — approximately 38.6% of rows have this set to true. Derived from the importance value.

onet_details_averaged.description: The number of O*NET detailed codes that contributed to this importance value. A value of 1 means the rating came from a single O*NET code (no averaging needed). Values of 2 or more mean the importance value is an unweighted average across multiple O*NET specializations that share the same BLS SOC. This provides transparency about aggregation quality.
onet_details_averaged.source_column: Derived: COUNT(DISTINCT onetsoc_code) per bls_soc_code, element_id group

suppress_flag.description: True if O*NET flagged any contributing Bronze source row as unreliable (recommend_suppress = 'Y'). When true, the importance value for this occupation-activity pair should be treated with caution. Expected to be true for less than 0.01% of rows in the current dataset. The data is preserved but flagged rather than dropped.
suppress_flag.source_column: Derived: logical OR of raw.onet_work_activities.recommend_suppress across contributing rows

source_load_date.description: The date this batch of O*NET data was loaded into the Bronze zone. Pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring.

ingested_at.description: The timestamp when this row was written to the Silver zone base table. Generated automatically at transformation time. Pipeline metadata.

base.onet_context_profiles.description: Work Context point-estimate measurements per occupation at BLS SOC granularity. Each row measures one of 57 Work Context dimensions for a given occupation, on the CX scale (1.0-5.0, 55 elements) or CT scale (1.0-3.0, 2 elements). This table backs the Burnout boss fight in the FutureProof game system, which assesses occupational stress and burnout risk. Nine elements are flagged as burnout-relevant (Time Pressure, Consequence of Error, etc.). Only CX/CT point-estimate scales are included — the CXP/CTP category-percentage scales (82% of Bronze Work Context data) remain in Bronze for post-hackathon use.

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the combination of BLS SOC code and element ID. This ID is stable across pipeline re-runs. Format: 'wc-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters.
record_id.source_column: Derived from bls_soc_code + element_id via compute_grain_id(prefix='wc')

bls_soc_code.description: The 6-digit BLS SOC code identifying which occupation this context measurement belongs to, in XX-XXXX format. Foreign key to base.onet_occupations.bls_soc_code. Combined with element_id, this forms the composite grain of the table. 774 distinct occupations have context profile data.
bls_soc_code.cde_rationale: FK to base.onet_occupations and primary join key for linking work context measurements to occupations. The Burnout boss fight keys on bls_soc_code to aggregate context values into occupation-level burnout scores. Incorrect values would assign context measurements to the wrong occupation, distorting Burnout scores.

element_id.description: The O*NET Content Model element identifier for the specific Work Context dimension being measured. Format is a dotted-hierarchical code like '4.C.3.d.1' (for 'Time Pressure'). There are exactly 57 distinct element IDs: 55 on the CX scale and 2 on the CT scale. These IDs are stable across O*NET releases. Gold zone Burnout boss fight filters on specific element_ids (the 9 burnout-relevant elements).
element_id.cde_rationale: Part of the composite grain and the identifier that uniquely distinguishes which of the 57 Work Context dimensions this row measures. Gold zone Burnout boss fight filters on specific element_ids to compute the Burnout score. Incorrect element_ids would include wrong context dimensions in Burnout calculations.
element_id.notes: Part of composite natural key. Exactly 57 distinct values (55 CX + 2 CT).

element_name.description: The human-readable name of the Work Context dimension, such as 'Time Pressure', 'Consequence of Error', or 'Duration of Typical Work Week'. This is a display label denormalized from Bronze — the element_id is the authoritative identifier.

scale_id.description: Identifies which O*NET measurement scale was used for this context value. 'CX' means a point estimate on a 1.0-5.0 scale (55 elements), 'CT' means a category value on a 1.0-3.0 scale (2 elements: Duration of Typical Work Week and Work Schedules). This field is essential for correctly interpreting context_value since the two scales have different ranges.
scale_id.source_column: raw.onet_work_context.scale_id (carried through, only CX and CT retained)

context_value.description: The point-estimate rating for this Work Context dimension and occupation. For CX scale elements, the range is 1.0 to 5.0 (55 elements). For CT scale elements, the range is 1.0 to 3.0 (2 elements). For multi-detail BLS SOCs, this is the unweighted average across all contributing O*NET detailed codes. This is the primary numeric input to the Burnout boss fight — for the 9 burnout-relevant elements, this value directly determines the occupation's burnout risk score.
context_value.cde_rationale: Primary numeric input to the Gold zone Burnout boss fight. For the 9 burnout-relevant elements, this value directly determines the occupation's burnout risk score. Also used in broader work environment profiling. Incorrect values would distort Burnout scores and work environment assessments presented to students.
context_value.source_column: AVG(raw.onet_work_context.data_value) WHERE scale_id IN ('CX','CT'), grouped by bls_soc_code and element_id
context_value.notes: CHECK constraint: >= 1.0 AND <= 5.0 (covers both CX and CT ranges). CX observed: 1.00-5.00. CT observed: 1.00-3.00.

is_burnout_element.description: True for the 9 Work Context elements identified as burnout-relevant. These elements measure occupational stress factors: Time Pressure, Consequence of Error, Pace Determined by Speed of Equipment, Frequency of Decision Making, Responsibility for Outcomes and Results, Importance of Being Exact or Accurate, Duration of Typical Work Week, Work Schedules, and two additional stress indicators. The Burnout boss fight formula weights these elements most heavily. The exact element IDs were validated against actual Bronze data by the EDA.
is_burnout_element.source_column: Derived: element_id IN (9 burnout-relevant IDs validated by EDA)
is_burnout_element.notes: Exactly 9 distinct element_ids flagged. EDA-corrected IDs differ from spec proposals on 4 of 9.

onet_details_averaged.description: The number of O*NET detailed codes that contributed to this context value. A value of 1 means the rating came from a single O*NET code. Values of 2 or more mean the context value is an unweighted average across multiple O*NET specializations. Transparency metadata for multi-detail aggregation.
onet_details_averaged.source_column: Derived: COUNT(DISTINCT onetsoc_code) per bls_soc_code, element_id group

suppress_flag.description: True if O*NET flagged any contributing Bronze source row as unreliable (recommend_suppress = 'Y'). When true, the context value for this occupation-element pair should be treated with caution. Expected to be true for less than 0.05% of rows. The data is preserved but flagged rather than dropped.
suppress_flag.source_column: Derived: logical OR of raw.onet_work_context.recommend_suppress across contributing rows

source_load_date.description: The date this batch of O*NET data was loaded into the Bronze zone. Pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring.

ingested_at.description: The timestamp when this row was written to the Silver zone base table. Generated automatically at transformation time. Pipeline metadata.

base.onet_career_transitions.description: Career similarity graph at BLS SOC granularity. Each row represents a directional relationship between two occupations indicating they share similar skill and knowledge profiles. Derived from O*NET's Related Occupations data, aggregated to BLS SOC level. This table powers the Stage 3 career branching tree in FutureProof, enabling exploration of alternative career paths. Important caveat: these are similarity-based relationships, not observed career transitions. 'Software Developer relates to Database Architect' means the jobs share similar profiles, not that developers commonly become architects.

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the combination of source and target BLS SOC codes. This ID is stable across pipeline re-runs. Format: 'ct-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters.
record_id.source_column: Derived from bls_soc_code + related_bls_soc_code via compute_grain_id(prefix='ct')

bls_soc_code.description: The 6-digit BLS SOC code identifying the source occupation in this career similarity relationship, in XX-XXXX format. Foreign key to base.onet_occupations.bls_soc_code. 'From this occupation, the related_bls_soc_code is a similar career.' Part of the composite grain.
bls_soc_code.notes: FK to base.onet_occupations. Part of composite natural key.

related_bls_soc_code.description: The 6-digit BLS SOC code identifying the target (related) occupation in this career similarity relationship, in XX-XXXX format. Foreign key to base.onet_occupations.bls_soc_code. Must differ from bls_soc_code (no self-references). Part of the composite grain.
related_bls_soc_code.notes: FK to base.onet_occupations. Must differ from bls_soc_code.

best_index.description: The relatedness index indicating how similar the two occupations are. Range: 1 (most similar) to 20 (least similar among related occupations). When multiple O*NET detail pairings produce the same BLS-level pair, this is the best (lowest) index — meaning the closest match wins. This drives the relatedness_tier classification.

relatedness_tier.description: A three-level classification of how closely related two occupations are, based on the best_index: 'Primary-Short' (index 1-5) means closest match, 'Primary-Long' (index 6-10) means moderately related, 'Supplemental' (index 11-20) means more distantly related. Used by the career branching tree to prioritize the most relevant alternative career paths.
relatedness_tier.source_column: Derived from best_index: 1-5 = Primary-Short, 6-10 = Primary-Long, 11-20 = Supplemental
relatedness_tier.notes: CHECK constraint: IN ('Primary-Short', 'Primary-Long', 'Supplemental'). Distribution: ~25.9% Primary-Short, ~24.7% Primary-Long, ~49.4% Supplemental.

is_primary.description: True when relatedness_tier is 'Primary-Short' or 'Primary-Long', meaning this is one of the occupation's closest career alternatives (best_index 1-10). False for 'Supplemental' relationships (best_index 11-20). A convenience filter flag for the career branching tree to show only the most relevant paths.

relationship_type.description: The type of career relationship. Currently always 'similarity' because the data source (O*NET Related Occupations) measures occupational similarity, not actual career transitions. If future O*NET releases include Career Changers/Starters transition matrices, this field would distinguish 'similarity' from 'transition' relationships without requiring schema changes.
relationship_type.notes: CHECK constraint: IN ('similarity'). Static for O*NET 30.2.

source_load_date.description: The date this batch of O*NET data was loaded into the Bronze zone. Pipeline metadata used for freshness monitoring.

ingested_at.description: The timestamp when this row was written to the Silver zone base table. Generated automatically at transformation time. Pipeline metadata.

raw.cip_soc_crosswalk.description: Raw ingest of the NCES CIP 2020 to SOC 2018 crosswalk, the authoritative bridge table connecting academic programs (CIP codes) to occupations (SOC codes). Each row represents one CIP-to-SOC pairing as defined by NCES and BLS statisticians. This is a many-to-many relationship: one program can lead to multiple occupations, and one occupation can be reached from multiple programs. Includes 194 'no match' sentinel rows (SOC 99-9999) representing programs with no corresponding occupation. This table is the foundation for connecting College Scorecard program data to BLS and O*NET occupation data in the Silver and Gold zones.

cipcode.description: The Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP) code identifying the academic program in this pairing. Format is XX.XXXX (e.g., '52.0201' for Business Administration and Management, General). This is a 6-digit code from the CIP 2020 taxonomy. IMPORTANT: Must always be treated as a string, never a float, because floating-point representation would lose leading zeros and trailing precision. Part of the composite grain with soc_code.
cipcode.cde_rationale: Composite natural key (1 of 2). CIP-side anchor of the crosswalk bridge table. Without correct cipcode values, the entire CIP-to-SOC integration chain breaks and no student query can reach occupation outcomes.
cipcode.source_column: CIP2020Code (XLSX column A, coerced from float to XX.XXXX string format)
cipcode.notes: 2,143 distinct CIP codes. All match XX.XXXX regex. Includes codes ranging from 01.0000 to 60.0599. Note: these are 6-digit CIP codes, while College Scorecard uses 4-digit (XX.XX). Zero direct joins are possible at this granularity.

cip_title.description: The human-readable name of the academic program corresponding to its CIP code, as provided by the NCES crosswalk source file. For example, 'Business Administration and Management, General.' for CIP 52.0201. This is a display label only and is not used for joins or matching.
cip_title.notes: 2,142 distinct values. One title ('Social Sciences, Other.') maps to two CIP codes (45.0199 and 45.9999).

soc_code.description: The Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) code identifying the occupation in this pairing. Format is XX-XXXX (e.g., '11-1021' for General and Operations Managers). Uses the SOC 2018 taxonomy. The special code '99-9999' is a 'no match' sentinel meaning the CIP program has no corresponding occupation per the NCES/BLS classification. Part of the composite grain with cipcode.
soc_code.cde_rationale: Composite natural key (2 of 2). SOC-side anchor of the crosswalk bridge table. Without correct soc_code values, BLS employment projections, wage data, and O*NET task profiles cannot be reached from any student query.
soc_code.notes: 868 distinct values including the 99-9999 sentinel. 867 valid occupation codes across 23 SOC major groups. 194 rows carry the 99-9999 sentinel.

soc_title.description: The human-readable name of the occupation corresponding to its SOC code, as provided by the NCES crosswalk source file. For example, 'General and Operations Managers' for SOC 11-1021. The sentinel value 'NO MATCH' appears for rows where soc_code is 99-9999. This is a display label only and is not used for joins or matching.
soc_title.notes: 868 distinct values in 1:1 correspondence with soc_code.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was written to the Bronze table by the pipeline. This is a pipeline metadata field generated automatically at ingestion time, not a value from the source data.
ingested_at.source_column: Generated at ingestion time via datetime.now()

source_url.description: The URL from which the crosswalk XLSX file was downloaded. This is the same for every row in a given load and exists for provenance tracking. Value: 'https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/cipcode/Files/CIP2020_SOC2018_Crosswalk.xlsx'.

source_method.description: Identifies how the source data was fetched. Always 'xlsx_download' for this table, indicating the data was ingested by downloading and parsing an XLSX file from the NCES website.

load_date.description: The date the source crosswalk XLSX was fetched and loaded into the Bronze table. This is a pipeline metadata field generated at ingestion time (date.today()), not a date from the source data.
load_date.source_column: Generated at ingestion time via date.today()

base.cip_soc_crosswalk.description: Clean, modeled crosswalk table connecting academic programs (CIP codes) to occupations (SOC codes). Each row represents one valid program-to-occupation pairing from the NCES CIP 2020 to SOC 2018 crosswalk, enriched with join-readiness flags indicating whether each pairing can connect to College Scorecard, BLS OOH, and O*NET data. The 'no match' sentinel rows (SOC 99-9999) are excluded. The match_quality field classifies each pairing's data completeness for downstream Gold products. CRITICAL CAVEAT: Under the current strict 6-digit CIP matching, has_scorecard_match is FALSE for 100% of rows because the crosswalk uses 6-digit CIP codes (XX.XXXX) while College Scorecard uses 4-digit (XX.XX). This is a known design limitation documented in the spec; resolution is deferred to the Gold zone.
base.cip_soc_crosswalk.source: raw.cip_soc_crosswalk (Bronze zone), with EXISTS lookups against base.college_scorecard, base.bls_ooh, and base.onet_occupations

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the combination of CIP code and SOC code. This ID is stable across pipeline re-runs -- the same CIP-SOC pair always produces the same record_id. Format: 'xw-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters (e.g., 'xw-a1b2c3d4e5f67890').
record_id.source_column: Derived from cipcode + soc_code via compute_grain_id(prefix='xw')
record_id.notes: Primary key. Deterministic SHA-256 hash truncated to 16 hex chars with 'xw' prefix.

cipcode.description: The Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP) code identifying the academic program in this pairing. Format is XX.XXXX (e.g., '52.0201' for Business Administration and Management, General). This is a 6-digit code from the CIP 2020 taxonomy and is one half of the composite natural key. Joins to base.college_scorecard.cipcode -- however, no direct matches currently exist due to the CIP granularity mismatch (6-digit crosswalk vs 4-digit Scorecard). Must always be treated as a string, never a float.
cipcode.cde_rationale: Composite natural key (1 of 2) and primary join key to base.college_scorecard.cipcode. CIP-side anchor of the crosswalk bridge table. Without correct cipcode values, the entire CIP-to-SOC integration chain breaks: no student query can reach occupation outcomes. Feeds all downstream Gold products that join programs to careers.
cipcode.notes: 1,949 distinct CIP codes after filtering 194 no-match sentinels. Natural key component. Zero direct matches to base.college_scorecard under strict 6-digit matching.

cip_title.description: The human-readable name of the academic program corresponding to its CIP code, sourced from the NCES crosswalk file. For example, 'Business Administration and Management, General.' for CIP 52.0201. This is a display label -- the cipcode field is the authoritative program identifier. Note: program names in this table may differ slightly from names in base.college_scorecard; Gold products should prefer the College Scorecard name when available.

cip_family.description: The broad discipline area that this program belongs to, represented as a 2-digit code derived from the first two characters of the CIP code. For example, '52' means Business, Management, Marketing. Useful for grouping programs by discipline. Matches base.college_scorecard.cip_family for cross-table aggregation.
cip_family.source_column: Derived from cipcode (first 2 characters, e.g., '52' from '52.0201')
cip_family.notes: 47 distinct CIP families observed in Bronze; fewer after filtering no-match rows.

soc_code.description: The Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) code identifying the occupation in this pairing. Format is XX-XXXX (e.g., '11-1021' for General and Operations Managers). Uses the SOC 2018 taxonomy. The 'no match' sentinel (99-9999) has been excluded from this table. This is one half of the composite natural key. Joins to base.bls_ooh.soc_code and base.onet_occupations.bls_soc_code.
soc_code.cde_rationale: Composite natural key (2 of 2) and primary join key to base.bls_ooh.soc_code and base.onet_occupations.bls_soc_code. SOC-side anchor of the crosswalk bridge table. Without correct soc_code values, BLS employment projections, wage data, and O*NET task profiles cannot be reached from any student query. Feeds all downstream Gold products that present career outcomes.
soc_code.notes: 867 distinct SOC codes after filtering. 820 (94.6%) match base.bls_ooh directly. 798 (92.0%) match base.onet_occupations.

soc_title.description: The human-readable name of the occupation corresponding to its SOC code, sourced from the NCES crosswalk file. For example, 'General and Operations Managers' for SOC 11-1021. This is a display label -- the soc_code field is the authoritative occupation identifier. Note: occupation names in this table may differ slightly from names in base.bls_ooh; Gold products should prefer the BLS OOH title when available.

soc_major_group.description: The broad occupational category this occupation belongs to, represented as a 2-digit code derived from the first two characters of the SOC code. For example, '11' means Management occupations, '29' means Healthcare Practitioners and Technical occupations. There are 22 valid civilian SOC major groups plus '55' for Military. Matches base.bls_ooh.soc_major_group for cross-table aggregation.
soc_major_group.source_column: Derived from soc_code (first 2 characters, e.g., '11' from '11-1021')
soc_major_group.notes: 22 distinct valid SOC major groups after filtering sentinel group 99. SOC group 25 (Education) dominates with 30.0% of rows.

has_scorecard_match.description: Indicates whether this CIP code exists in at least one row of base.college_scorecard. TRUE means there is College Scorecard program data (earnings, debt, completers) available for this academic program. FALSE means students who pick this program in the crosswalk will not have Scorecard outcome data. IMPORTANT: Under the current strict 6-digit CIP matching, this flag is FALSE for 100% of rows due to the CIP granularity mismatch (crosswalk uses XX.XXXX, Scorecard uses XX.XX). This is a known limitation; the Gold zone will resolve it via 4-digit fallback matching.
has_scorecard_match.source_column: Derived via EXISTS lookup against base.college_scorecard on cipcode
has_scorecard_match.notes: 0% TRUE (0 of 5,903 rows) under strict 6-digit matching. EDA estimates 82% TRUE if 4-digit matching were used.

has_bls_match.description: Indicates whether this SOC code exists in base.bls_ooh. TRUE means there is BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook data available for this occupation, including employment projections, median wages, and growth outlook. FALSE means this occupation lacks BLS projection data, typically because the crosswalk uses a more detailed SOC code than BLS reports at.
has_bls_match.source_column: Derived via EXISTS lookup against base.bls_ooh on soc_code
has_bls_match.notes: 97.39% TRUE (5,749 of 5,903 rows). 47 distinct SOC codes not found in BLS OOH due to SOC version granularity differences.

has_onet_match.description: Indicates whether this SOC code exists in base.onet_occupations (matched on bls_soc_code). TRUE means there is O*NET task-level data available for this occupation, including work activities, work context, and related occupations. FALSE means this occupation lacks O*NET profiling, typically because it is a residual 'All Other' category that O*NET does not profile individually.
has_onet_match.source_column: Derived via EXISTS lookup against base.onet_occupations on bls_soc_code
has_onet_match.notes: 94.85% TRUE (5,599 of 5,903 rows). 69 distinct SOC codes not found in O*NET -- all are 'All Other' residual categories.

match_quality.description: A classification of how complete the data is for this program-to-occupation pairing. Determines which FutureProof statistics can be computed. Values: 'full' = program data + BLS projections + O*NET tasks (all stats available); 'partial_no_onet' = program data + BLS but no O*NET (missing HMN and Burnout scores); 'partial_no_bls' = program data + O*NET but no BLS (missing growth and market scores); 'scorecard_only' = program data only (earnings/ROI but no occupation-level stats); 'no_scorecard' = no College Scorecard data (pair exists in crosswalk but no student queries will reach it). IMPORTANT: Under strict 6-digit CIP matching, 100% of rows are 'no_scorecard'. With 4-digit matching (Gold zone), an estimated 76.6% would be 'full'.
match_quality.cde_rationale: Business-critical classification that determines which FutureProof statistics can be computed for each program-occupation pair. Downstream Gold products use match_quality to decide whether to compute earnings, growth, O*NET task profiles, or flag incomplete data. Incorrect match_quality values would cause Gold products to either omit available data or present incomplete data without disclosure. Drives confidence scoring and data completeness transparency for end users.
match_quality.source_column: Derived from has_scorecard_match, has_bls_match, has_onet_match via 5-tier CASE expression
match_quality.notes: Under strict matching: 100% no_scorecard. EDA Scenario B (4-digit matching): 76.6% full, 3.1% partial_no_onet, 1.4% partial_no_bls, 0.8% scorecard_only, 18.0% no_scorecard.

source_load_date.description: The date the crosswalk source data was originally loaded into the Bronze zone. Renamed from the Bronze field 'load_date' for clarity. This is a pipeline metadata field, not a date from the NCES source data.

ingested_at.description: The exact date and time when this row was written to the Silver base table by the transformer. This is generated at Silver transformation time and is independent of the Bronze ingested_at timestamp.
ingested_at.source_column: Generated at Silver transformation time via CURRENT_TIMESTAMP

consumable.onet_work_profiles.description: The occupation work profiles data product for FutureProof. Each row represents one U.S. occupation identified by its BLS SOC code, enriched with the Human Edge (HMN) score measuring how much the occupation relies on distinctly human skills, the Burnout score measuring occupational burnout risk, activity and context profile summaries, and data quality context. This table powers the HMN pentagon stat, the AI and Burnout boss fights, and Gemma's human-edge and burnout narratives. 798 occupations total: 774 with full scores, 24 with partial data (scores null).

record_id.description: A unique identifier for each row, computed automatically from the BLS SOC code using a deterministic hash function. This ID is stable -- running the pipeline again on the same data produces the same ID for the same row. Format: 'wp-' followed by 16 hexadecimal characters. The 'wp' prefix distinguishes Gold work profile records from Silver O*NET records (which use 'on', 'wa', 'wc', 'ct' prefixes).

bls_soc_code.description: The Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) code that uniquely identifies this occupation. Format is XX-XXXX, where the first two digits identify the major occupation group and the last four identify the specific occupation. This is the natural key of the table and the primary join key for connecting to consumable.occupation_profiles (BLS OOH data), consumable.career_transitions (similarity graph), and the future CIP-to-SOC crosswalk. 798 unique values.
bls_soc_code.cde_rationale: Natural key and primary join anchor for the consumable work profiles table. Every downstream consumer queries by bls_soc_code: Gemma agent lookups, cross-table joins to occupation_profiles and career_transitions, pentagon stat rendering for HMN, Burnout boss fight engine, and Stage 3 branching.

primary_title.description: The occupation title from O*NET. For example, 'Software Developers' or 'Registered Nurses.' This is the display label used by Gemma for career descriptions. The bls_soc_code field, not this title, is the authoritative identifier.

description.description: The occupation description from O*NET, providing a functional summary of what people in this occupation do. Used by Gemma for career context but not for scoring or joins.

multi_detail_flag.description: True for 76 occupations where multiple O*NET detailed codes (XX-XXXX.XX) were averaged to produce the BLS-level SOC (XX-XXXX) ratings. When true, the activity and context values are averages across multiple O*NET specializations within the same BLS occupation.

data_completeness_tier.description: Whether this occupation has full or partial O*NET data coverage. 'full' means all four O*NET data types (activities, context, tasks, related occupations) are available -- 774 occupations. 'partial' means only some data types are present -- 24 occupations. Partial-data occupations will have null HMN and Burnout scores.

hmn_score.description: The Human Edge stat on a 1-10 scale, measuring how much this occupation relies on distinctly human skills that are difficult for AI to replicate -- things like interpersonal judgment, creative problem-solving, physical presence, emotional intelligence, and leadership. A score of 10 means nearly all of the occupation's important work activities are human-intensive; a score of 1 means the work is mostly automatable. Derived by computing the ratio of importance-weighted human-intensive activities to total activity importance, then mapping to a 1-10 scale. Null for 24 partial-data occupations that lack activity profiles.
hmn_score.cde_rationale: Primary scored output for the HMN pentagon stat. Consumed by the game engine, Gemma agent, and AI boss fight context. An incorrect hmn_score would directly misrepresent an occupation's human edge potential to students.
hmn_score.source_column: Derived from base.onet_activity_profiles importance values via human_ratio formula: 1.0 + 9.0 * human_ratio

hmn_score_rounded.description: The Human Edge stat rounded to the nearest integer (1-10) for pentagon display in the FutureProof game engine. Always equals ROUND(hmn_score). Null when hmn_score is null.

top_human_activities.description: A JSON array listing the top 5 most important human-intensive work activities for this occupation, with their importance ratings. For example, a nurse might show activities like 'Assisting and Caring for Others' and 'Establishing and Maintaining Interpersonal Relationships.' This powers Gemma's narrative about what the human edge looks like in practice for a given career. Null when no activity profile is available.
top_human_activities.source_column: Derived from base.onet_activity_profiles (human-intensive subset, top 5 by importance)

human_activity_count.description: The number of human-intensive activities (out of 14 classified) that have data for this occupation. Always 14 when activity data is present. Null when no activity profile is available.
human_activity_count.source_column: Derived: count of 14 human-intensive activities with data

burnout_score.description: The Burnout risk stat on a 1-10 scale, measuring how much occupational stress and burnout risk this career involves. Higher scores mean more burnout risk. Derived by normalizing 9 burnout-relevant Work Context elements (time pressure, work hours, consequence of error, pace determined by equipment, decision frequency, precision requirements, responsibility for outcomes, task repetition, and responsibility for others' safety) and computing their average, then mapping to a 1-10 scale. A score of 10 means maximum stress across all indicators; 1 means minimal burnout risk. Null for 24 partial-data occupations.
burnout_score.cde_rationale: Primary scored output for the Burnout boss fight. Consumed by the game engine for boss difficulty calibration, by Gemma for burnout narrative, and by the compare screen. An incorrect burnout_score would miscalibrate the Burnout boss fight.
burnout_score.source_column: Derived from base.onet_context_profiles (9 burnout elements) via normalized average: 1.0 + 9.0 * weighted_average

burnout_score_rounded.description: The Burnout risk stat rounded to the nearest integer (1-10) for boss fight display in the FutureProof game engine. Always equals ROUND(burnout_score). Null when burnout_score is null.

burnout_drivers.description: A JSON array listing the top 3 Work Context elements that contribute most to this occupation's burnout score, with their normalized values. For example, a nurse might show 'Consequence of Error,' 'Responsibility for Others Health and Safety,' and 'Time Pressure.' This tells students WHY an occupation has its burnout risk level, not just the score. Null when burnout_score is null.
